classifier.name: Environment Classifier
classifier.description: Routes the assessment to the correct question track. Not scored.

questions.text: Where would AI primarily operate in your organization?
options: Inside software, digital tools, or knowledge workflows
Inside physical equipment, machines, or robotic systems
Inside scientific research, modeling, or discovery processes
Across real-time operational systems like logistics, infrastructure, or facilities

questions.text: If AI made a wrong decision in your environment, what is the most likely consequence?
options: A bad recommendation a human can review and correct
A flawed research result or model prediction
A disrupted process, system outage, or missed SLA
A physical event — equipment damage, safety incident, or regulatory violation

sections.name: Data Readiness
sections.description: How ready is the organization's data?

questions.text: How organized and accessible is your current data?

workflow.text: How organized and centralized is your current business data across tools and systems?
options: No centralized data — everything lives in emails and memory
Partially organized across multiple disconnected tools
Mostly organized with some silos remaining
Well-organized with minimal silos and good accessibility
Fully centralized, documented, and consistently maintained

scientific.text: How is your experimental or research data currently managed and stored?
options: Lab notebooks and informal records only
Spreadsheets with inconsistent structure
Structured databases with some gaps in documentation
Versioned, structured datasets with metadata
Fully governed data pipelines with reproducibility standards

operational.text: How consistently does your operational environment generate and store event or sensor data?
options: No systematic data collection from operations
Sporadic data collection, mostly manual
Continuous collection but unstructured and hard to query
Continuous, structured, and queryable in near real-time
Fully instrumented with automated pipelines and historical depth

embedded.text: What is the quality and consistency of sensor and telemetry data from your physical systems?
options: No sensor data collected or logged
Sensor data collected but unreliable and inconsistent
Generally reliable data with known gaps
Clean, timestamped data from most systems
High-fidelity, labeled, timestamped data across all systems with redundancy

questions.text: What is the quality and consistency of your data?

workflow.text: What is the quality and consistency of your business data across systems?
options: Poor quality — duplicates, missing fields, and no standards
Low quality with frequent inconsistencies across tools
Moderate quality with known issues being addressed
Good quality, mostly consistent with occasional gaps
Excellent quality — validated, consistent, and well-maintained

scientific.text: How validated and reproducible is your research data?
options: No validation standards — results are hard to reproduce
Minimal validation, reproducibility is inconsistent
Basic validation protocols in place
Formal validation with documented methodology
Rigorous validation with full reproducibility and peer review standards

operational.text: How reliable and clean is your operational data for real-time decision making?
options: Data is too noisy or incomplete to act on
Useful in aggregate but unreliable for real-time use
Reliable enough for batch analysis but not live inference
Clean and reliable for most real-time use cases
High-fidelity, anomaly-filtered, and validated for live AI inference

embedded.text: How suitable is your sensor and hardware data for training and running AI models?
options: Raw, unlabeled, and unsuitable for model training
Partially labeled with significant noise and gaps
Labeled for some scenarios but inconsistent across conditions
Well-labeled across most operating conditions
Comprehensive labeled datasets covering edge cases and failure modes

questions.text: Do you have data governance and management policies in place?

workflow.text: Do you have data governance policies that define how business data is collected, stored, and accessed?
options: No data governance policies exist
Informal rules but nothing documented or enforced
Basic policies in place for key systems
Comprehensive policies that are actively enforced
Advanced governance with auditing, lineage tracking, and continuous improvement

scientific.text: Do you have data management standards that ensure research integrity and IP protection?
options: No formal data management standards
Informal practices dependent on individual researchers
Basic standards for data storage and sharing
Formal standards with IP protection and access controls
Institution-grade governance with regulatory compliance and audit trails

operational.text: Do you have policies governing how operational data is collected, retained, and used for automated decisions?
options: No formal policies — data use is ad hoc
Minimal policies, inconsistently applied
Basic retention and access policies in place
Formal policies with defined data retention and usage rules
Comprehensive governance including automated decision audit trails

embedded.text: Do you have data governance policies that address AI model inputs, outputs, and failure logging in physical systems?
options: No governance for AI system data
Informal logging with no structured policies
Basic input/output logging for key systems
Formal policies with failure logging and model versioning
Full lifecycle governance including regulatory compliance and safety audit trails

sections.name: Infrastructure and Integration
sections.description: Can your infrastructure handle widespread AI integration?

questions.text: How scalable is your current infrastructure for AI workloads?

workflow.text: How scalable is your current IT infrastructure for adding AI-powered software tools?
options: Not scalable — significant technical limitations
Limited scalability — would require major investment
Moderate scalability with some constraints
Highly scalable with room for expansion
Enterprise-grade scalability with cloud-ready architecture

scientific.text: Do you have access to the compute infrastructure needed for model training and large-scale data processing?
options: No access to meaningful compute resources
Basic cloud access only — no GPU or HPC capability
Shared HPC access or moderate cloud GPU availability
Dedicated GPU infrastructure or scalable cloud cluster
Purpose-built HPC environment with elastic scaling for large model training

operational.text: Can your current infrastructure handle real-time data ingestion and low-latency AI inference at operational scale?
options: No — infrastructure was not designed for real-time workloads
Partial capability with significant latency constraints
Adequate for moderate throughput but not peak load
Mostly capable with identified bottlenecks being addressed
Purpose-built for real-time AI inference with redundancy and failover

embedded.text: Do your physical systems have the edge compute capacity and connectivity to run AI inference onboard?
options: No — systems have no onboard processing for AI
Limited — a few devices could support lightweight models
Partial — some systems are edge-capable with constraints
Most systems can support edge inference with acceptable latency
Full edge AI capability with redundancy, OTA updates, and failsafe fallback

questions.text: How well can your systems integrate with AI tools and platforms?

workflow.text: What level of API and integration capability do your current business systems have?
options: No API integration capability
Basic API access on a few tools
Standard APIs across most core systems
Advanced integration with webhooks and event-driven architecture
Fully integrated ecosystem with real-time data flow across all tools

scientific.text: How well do your research tools and data systems integrate with AI and ML platforms?
options: No integration — tools are entirely siloed
Manual data export/import between tools
Some programmatic integration via scripts
API-connected tools with reproducible pipelines
Fully automated ML pipelines from raw data to model deployment

operational.text: How well do your operational systems integrate with data platforms and AI decision engines?
options: No integration — operational data is inaccessible to AI tools
Manual data extraction only
Batch integration running on a schedule
Near real-time integration for most critical systems
Full bidirectional integration with AI feedback loops into operations

embedded.text: How well do your physical systems communicate with AI platforms for model updates, monitoring, and feedback?
options: No connectivity — systems are fully air-gapped without a plan
Manual updates only — no remote management
Scheduled connectivity for batch updates
OTA update capability with basic telemetry
Continuous telemetry, remote model updates, and anomaly alerting with rollback

questions.text: How robust is your cybersecurity posture for AI workloads?

workflow.text: How robust is your cybersecurity and data protection infrastructure for AI-powered software tools?
options: Minimal security measures — no formal program
Basic security in place but inconsistently applied
Adequate protocols with known gaps being addressed
Strong security framework with regular assessments
Enterprise-level security with compliance certification and AI-specific controls

scientific.text: How well protected is your research environment against data theft, model exfiltration, and IP exposure?
options: No formal protection for research data or models
Basic access controls on storage systems
Formal access management with some monitoring
Strong IP protection with data classification and audit logs
Research-grade security with air-gapped environments for sensitive work

operational.text: How protected are your operational systems against AI-driven errors, adversarial inputs, or cyberattack?
options: No controls specific to AI or operational system threats
Basic network segmentation only
Anomaly detection on critical operational systems
Formal threat modeling with AI-specific controls
OT/IT security convergence with AI threat monitoring and incident response

embedded.text: How do you protect AI models and physical systems from tampering, adversarial inputs, or unauthorized updates?
options: No security controls on deployed AI or physical systems
Basic physical security only
Signed firmware and access-controlled update channels
Formal security testing including adversarial robustness evaluation
Defense-in-depth with hardware security modules, secure boot, and red team testing

sections.name: Human Capital and AI Literacy
sections.description: How proficient and AI-literate is your team?

questions.text: What is your team's current understanding of AI capabilities and limitations?

workflow.text: What is your team's current understanding of AI tools, their capabilities, and where they fail?
options: Minimal — most staff have never used an AI tool
Basic awareness — familiar with consumer AI but not business applications
Good understanding of AI tools relevant to our work
Strong understanding including limitations and failure modes
Expert-level — team can evaluate, prompt, and critically assess AI outputs

scientific.text: Does your team have the ML and data science expertise to build, validate, and interpret AI models?
options: No ML or data science expertise on the team
One person with basic exposure to ML concepts
Some capability — can use existing tools and frameworks
Strong capability — can train, validate, and tune models
Dedicated AI/ML research capability with domain-specific expertise

operational.text: Does your team include people who understand both your operational domain and AI/ML systems?
options: No — operational and technical staff are entirely separate
Minimal overlap — one or two people with adjacent knowledge
Some cross-functional capability being developed
A small team that bridges operational and AI expertise
Dedicated operational AI team with domain and ML depth

embedded.text: Does your team have experience in embedded systems, edge AI, or hardware-software integration for AI?
options: No embedded systems or edge AI experience
Traditional embedded systems experience but no AI integration
Some experience deploying ML models on constrained hardware
Solid embedded AI capability — can design and deploy edge inference systems
Deep expertise in edge AI including custom silicon, safety certification, and real-time systems

questions.text: Do you have staff with the technical expertise needed for AI in your environment?

workflow.text: Do you have staff who can manage, configure, and evaluate AI tools in a business context?
options: No technical expertise relevant to AI tools
Very limited — one person exploring on their own
Some capability — can implement tools with vendor support
Good technical team that can configure and manage AI tools independently
Dedicated AI operations or data team with full implementation capability

scientific.text: Do you have the domain-specific expertise to train AI models on your research data without introducing bias or error?
options: No — we lack both domain and ML expertise
Domain expertise exists but no ML capability to apply it
ML capability exists but domain experts aren't involved in model design
Domain experts and ML practitioners collaborate on model development
Fully integrated team where domain expertise directly shapes model architecture and validation

operational.text: Do you have engineers who can build and maintain AI systems that interface with your operational environment?
options: No engineering capability for AI integration
Software engineers with no operational systems experience
Some engineers learning operational AI integration
Engineers who can build and deploy operational AI with guidance
Full team capable of designing, deploying, and maintaining operational AI systems

embedded.text: Do you have engineers who can design AI systems that meet the safety, latency, and reliability requirements of your physical environment?
options: No — we lack the engineering depth for embedded AI
Traditional hardware engineers with no AI background
Software AI engineers without embedded constraints experience
Engineers who can optimize models for embedded constraints
Systems engineers who design for safety certification, real-time performance, and hardware-AI co-design

questions.text: How receptive is your workforce to AI-driven changes?

workflow.text: How receptive is your workforce to AI-driven changes in their daily tools and workflows?
options: Highly resistant — strong cultural pushback
Mostly resistant — concern about job displacement
Neutral or mixed — some enthusiasm, some skepticism
Generally accepting with good change management support
Enthusiastically supportive — team sees AI as a capability multiplier

scientific.text: How open is your research team to incorporating AI and ML into their scientific methodology?
options: Resistant — concern about AI undermining scientific rigor
Skeptical — willing to observe but not adopt
Curious but cautious — open to validated use cases
Generally supportive — actively exploring AI-assisted research
Fully embracing — AI is already part of the research methodology

operational.text: How comfortable are your operations teams with AI systems making or informing operational decisions?
options: Not comfortable — strong preference for human-only decision making
Cautious — willing to use AI as an advisory tool only
Neutral — open to AI if reliability is demonstrated
Comfortable with AI-assisted decisions on well-understood processes
Confident — operations team trusts and actively relies on AI-driven insights

embedded.text: How comfortable are your engineering and operations teams with AI running autonomously in physical systems?
options: Not comfortable — no trust in autonomous AI decision making
Cautious — only acceptable with constant human oversight
Conditional — acceptable in low-risk, well-tested scenarios
Comfortable in defined operational envelopes with monitoring
Confident — team has experience validating and trusting autonomous AI in physical systems

sections.name: Leadership Readiness
sections.description: How AI-literate is your leadership and what is their level of commitment?

questions.text: How well does your leadership understand AI's strategic importance in your context?

workflow.text: How well does your leadership understand how AI can create competitive advantage in your industry?
options: No strategic understanding — AI is seen as a cost center
Minimal awareness — leadership has heard the buzz but lacks depth
Moderate understanding — recognizes opportunity but lacks a plan
Strong strategic vision — AI is part of the business roadmap
Clear AI strategy with defined use cases, owners, and success metrics

scientific.text: How well does your leadership understand what AI-assisted research can and cannot do for your scientific mission?
options: No understanding — leadership is not engaged with AI in research
Aware of AI in science broadly but no domain-specific understanding
Understands the opportunity but unclear on implementation requirements
Has a defined vision for AI in research with resource commitment
AI research strategy is formally documented with validation protocols and ethics review

operational.text: How well does your leadership understand the operational risk and opportunity of deploying AI in your systems?
options: No understanding of operational AI risk or opportunity
Aware of AI operationally but no risk framework in place
Understands the opportunity but has not addressed failure scenarios
Has defined acceptable risk thresholds and a deployment strategy
Formal operational AI governance with risk tolerance, SLAs, and escalation paths

embedded.text: Does your leadership understand the safety, regulatory, and liability implications of deploying AI in physical systems?
options: No awareness of safety or regulatory requirements for embedded AI
Aware of compliance requirements but no formal response
Has identified relevant standards but not yet addressed them
Safety and regulatory requirements are documented and being addressed
Full safety case developed with legal, regulatory, and insurance considerations resolved

questions.text: What is your organization's financial commitment to AI initiatives?

workflow.text: What is your organization's financial commitment to AI tools and implementation?
options: No budget allocated for AI
Minimal budget — exploring free or low-cost tools only
Moderate budget for tools and some implementation support
Significant budget with dedicated AI spend line
Substantial ongoing investment with multi-year AI roadmap funding

scientific.text: Has your organization committed funding for compute infrastructure, data preparation, and ML talent for AI-assisted research?
options: No funding for AI in research
Exploratory only — using free resources or shared infrastructure
Project-level funding for specific AI research initiatives
Program-level funding with dedicated compute and personnel
Strategic investment — AI is a funded research priority with infrastructure roadmap

operational.text: Has your organization committed funding for the infrastructure, integration, and monitoring required for operational AI?
options: No funding for operational AI
Pilot-only funding — not committed to production deployment
Moderate funding for specific operational AI projects
Significant investment in operational AI infrastructure
Full program investment with operational AI embedded in capital planning

embedded.text: Has your organization committed funding for the hardware, certification, and engineering required to deploy AI in physical systems?
options: No funding for embedded AI
R&D exploration only — no production commitment
Prototype-level funding for specific embedded AI use cases
Product-level funding with safety and certification budget included
Full program investment including hardware development, certification, and lifecycle maintenance

questions.text: How committed is leadership to supporting the organizational change required for AI adoption?

workflow.text: How committed is leadership to supporting process change, retraining, and workflow redesign for AI adoption?
options: No commitment — expecting AI to work without changing anything
Minimal support — willing to fund tools but not change management
Moderate support — recognizes change is needed, starting to address it
Strong commitment with change management resources allocated
Fully committed with active executive sponsorship and transformation program

scientific.text: How committed is research leadership to integrating AI into scientific workflows and retraining researchers?
options: No commitment — AI adoption left to individual researchers
Minimal — leadership endorses it but provides no resources
Moderate — supporting pilot programs and voluntary adoption
Strong — AI integration is a stated research priority with training programs
Fully committed — AI methodology is being institutionalized with updated research standards

operational.text: How committed is operations leadership to redesigning workflows and retraining staff around AI-driven decision making?
options: No commitment to operational change
Willing to add AI on top of existing workflows only
Open to workflow redesign in specific areas
Committed to systematic workflow redesign with staff involvement
Fully committed — operational transformation is an executive priority with change program

embedded.text: How committed is leadership to the engineering investment, safety validation, and long-term support required for embedded AI in physical systems?
options: Not committed — treating embedded AI as a short-term experiment
Interested but not resourced for the full engineering requirement
Committed to prototype and pilot phases only
Committed to production deployment with safety validation budgeted
Fully committed including post-deployment monitoring, model maintenance, and regulatory engagement

sections.name: Security Assessment
sections.description: What is your security posture for AI implementation?

questions.text: How well does your organization handle data privacy and compliance for AI?

workflow.text: How well does your organization handle data privacy and regulatory compliance when using AI tools?
options: No privacy framework — data is used in AI tools without review
Basic compliance awareness but no formal controls
Adequate protocols — reviewed key tools for compliance
Strong privacy and compliance with documented AI data policies
Advanced compliance framework including AI-specific data processing agreements

scientific.text: How well does your organization protect research participant data, proprietary findings, and IP when using AI platforms?
options: No formal protection — research data flows freely into AI tools
Basic awareness but no formal controls for research data
IRB and IP policies exist but don't address AI specifically
Formal policies governing research data use in AI with legal review
Comprehensive framework covering IRB compliance, IP protection, and AI data processing

operational.text: How well does your organization manage regulatory compliance and data sovereignty for AI in operational systems?
options: No compliance framework for operational AI
Aware of relevant regulations but not yet compliant
Compliance addressed for primary regulatory requirements
Strong compliance posture with documented controls for operational AI
Proactive compliance including regulatory engagement and future-proofing for emerging AI regulations

embedded.text: How well does your organization address the regulatory, certification, and liability requirements for AI in physical systems?
options: No regulatory awareness for embedded AI
Aware of requirements but no formal compliance program
Partial compliance — addressing some but not all requirements
Formal compliance program covering primary certification requirements
Full regulatory compliance with safety case documentation, third-party audit, and insurance coverage

questions.text: What is your incident response and recovery capability for AI system failures?

workflow.text: What is your incident response capability if an AI tool produces harmful outputs or fails in a business-critical workflow?
options: No formal processes — would improvise
Basic processes but not tested for AI-specific scenarios
Adequate response procedures with some AI failure scenarios covered
Well-defined processes with AI failure runbooks
Robust incident response with regular AI-specific tabletop exercises

scientific.text: What is your capability to detect, investigate, and recover from AI model failures or research data corruption?
options: No formal processes for model failure or data corruption
Basic backup and recovery for data only
Versioned data and model checkpoints with basic recovery process
Formal incident process covering data corruption, model failure, and result retraction
Comprehensive scientific integrity framework with automated anomaly detection and recovery protocols

operational.text: What is your incident response capability if an AI system causes an operational failure or makes a consequential wrong decision?
options: No formal processes for AI-driven operational failures
General IT incident response only — not tailored to AI
Basic runbooks for known AI failure modes
Well-defined AI incident response with human override protocols
Robust operational resilience framework with AI-specific failure modes, SLAs, and automated fallback

embedded.text: What is your capability to detect, respond to, and recover from AI failures in deployed physical systems?
options: No formal processes — physical system failures handled reactively
Manual inspection and recovery only
Remote diagnostics with manual recovery procedures
Automated fault detection with defined safe-state fallback behavior
Full safety system with automated fault isolation, fail-safe modes, and remote recovery capability

questions.text: How do you manage AI model risks and ethical considerations?

workflow.text: How does your organization manage the risk of AI bias, hallucination, and unintended outputs in business workflows?
options: No formal risk management for AI outputs
Minimal — we rely on vendor claims about model safety
Basic output review process for high-stakes AI decisions
Formal AI risk management with bias testing and human-in-the-loop for critical decisions
Comprehensive AI ethics framework with ongoing monitoring, bias audits, and stakeholder accountability

scientific.text: How does your organization manage the risk of AI-introduced bias, overfitting, or error in scientific findings?
options: No formal process for managing AI model risk in research
Rely on standard statistical review without AI-specific checks
Basic model validation against held-out test sets
Formal validation protocol with bias testing and independent review
Rigorous AI research governance including adversarial testing, reproducibility requirements, and ethics board review

operational.text: How does your organization manage the risk of AI making consequential wrong decisions in operational contexts?
options: No formal AI risk management for operational decisions
Rely on model accuracy metrics without operational risk framing
Basic thresholds and human review for high-confidence decisions only
Formal operational risk framework with defined confidence thresholds and escalation paths
Comprehensive AI risk governance including decision audit trails, bias monitoring, and continuous model performance review

embedded.text: How does your organization manage the safety risk of AI making wrong decisions in physical systems where consequences are real and immediate?
options: No formal safety risk management for embedded AI
Rely on software testing only — no hardware-level safety analysis
Basic safety analysis completed for primary failure modes
Formal safety case developed using established methodology such as FMEA or HAZOP
Full functional safety framework with independent safety assessment, redundancy design, and regulatory sign-off
